relationship_to_restored_text: Interpretive contemplative layer aligned by verse. This does not replace the source-audited restored translation.

verses.mystical_translation: The vineyard is planted, protected, entrusted, and left in human care.
Fruit is sought in its season.
The servant is rejected and sent away empty.
Another servant is dishonored.
The pattern of rejecting messengers continues.
The beloved son is sent last.
The heir exposes the tenants’ desire to possess what is not theirs.
The son is cast outside the vineyard.
Fruitless tenants lose the vineyard.
The rejected stone becomes the cornerstone.
What the Lord does is marvelous.
They understand the parable and fear the crowd.
Pharisees and Herodians come to trap him in speech.
They ask about Caesar under the cover of flattery.
Jesus names the hypocrisy and asks for a coin.
Image and inscription reveal Caesar’s claim.
Give Caesar what bears Caesar’s image and the Divine what bears divine image.
Sadducees question resurrection from denial.
They cite Moses and levirate duty.
The seven-brother scenario begins.
The chain continues.
The woman also dies.
They use the question to flatten resurrection into present arrangements.
Jesus says they miss Scripture and divine power.
Resurrection life is not merely earthly marriage extended.
The bush passage speaks the living Divine of the patriarchs.
The Divine is not God of the dead but of the living.
A scribe asks for the first command.
Jesus begins with the Shema: the Lord is one.
Love of the Divine gathers heart, life, mind, and strength.
Neighbor-love stands beside it.
The scribe receives the truth of divine oneness.
Love is greater than offerings and sacrifices.
Thoughtful answer places him near the reign.
Jesus asks how Messiah is David’s son.
David speaks in holy Spirit of Lord and right hand.
Messiah is more than a simple lineage category.
Jesus warns against religious status performance.
First seats reveal the appetite for honor.
Long prayers can hide exploitation of widows.
Jesus watches the treasury and the giving crowd.
A poor widow gives two small coins.
Jesus says she gave more than all.
She gives from lack: her whole living.
